Non-rudimentary facets, such as aquatic pursuits and athletics, can still allure a diminutive cohort of visitors, provided they possess admittance to these verdant realms.

title: Revenge, Redemption, and Rhythm: The Language of Gendered Violence in Colombian Popular Lyrics
abstract: This study explores gendered violence, emotional expression, and performative identities in the top 200 popular songs from Colombia on Spotify between 2017 and 2021, focusing on the Spanish lyrical content of solo and collaborative artists. Hypothesis 1 reveals that male collaborations use more derogatory language than male solo artists, amplifying violence through group dynamics. Hypothesis 2 shows that anger, as an automatic tool to assert dominance among Colombian male artists, aligns with Raewyn Connell’s concept of hegemonic masculinity. Hypothesis 3 reflects Judith Butler’s theory of gender performativity, demonstrating how women embody societal scripts of nurturing and compliance shaped by the male gaze. These findings support Veena Das’s notion of language as a tool of everyday violence.The results emphasize the role of popular music and culture in perpetuating gendered narratives and societal norms in Colombia. Future research should investigate emotional expression in Spanish-language lyrics, assess the severity of derogatory language, and examine the cultural construction of female desire. This work advances the understanding of how gender, power, and culture intersect in musical expression.
Author: Chowdhury, Tanzima
abstract_manipulated: Pursuant to the revelatory implications inherent within, this interrogation epistemologically scrutinizes gendered aggression, affective articulation, and theatric personifications within the apex 200 mellifluous compositions from Colombia upon Spotify spanning the years 2017 to 2021, centering on the Hispanophone lyrical substance of individualistic and cooperative virtuosos. Hypothesis 1 proffers that male conjunctures employ a higher frequency of pejorative vernacular compared to male solitaires, thereby magnifying aggression via the collective synergies. Hypothesis 2 elucidates that ire, as an automatized implement for dominance assertion amidst Colombian male minstrels, aligns with Raewyn Connell’s construct of hegemonic virility. Hypothesis 3 mirrors Judith Butler’s paradigm of gender enactment, elucidating how feminines embody societal dramaturgies of nurturance and acquiescence enwrought by the masculine scrutiny. These revelations serve to illuminate the extent to which language functions as an instrument of quotidian brutality. The outcomes underscore the function of prevailing melodies and traditions in perpetuating gendered narrations and communal dictates within Colombia. Subsequent inquiries should undertake the assessment of emotive articulation in Hispano-lingual verses, gauge the gravity of pejorative dialect, and scrutinize the societal configuration of feminine longing. This undertaking propels the comprehension of how gender, authority, and tradition intersect in melodic articulation.

title: Longshot Bias — Evidence from Betting on UK Premier League Matches
abstract: This study explores the longshot bias phenomenon in the context of soccer betting, focusing on the betting behaviors of English Premier League fans in Kenya and the United Kingdom. Using data from two field experiments involving 2,410 participants collected for a separate study (Donkor et al., 2023), this research investigates how subjective beliefs and preferences influence bettors’ decisions, revealing that both biased beliefs and preferences contribute to the longshot bias. Participants tend to underestimate the win probabilities of favored teams and overestimate those of underdogs, affecting their betting allocations accordingly. Conditional on betting odds and subjective beliefs, participants also tend to allocate smaller (larger) bets on favored (underdog) teams winning a given match. This research underscores the importance of understanding and addressing the underlying cognitive and psychological factors driving longshot bias to foster more rational betting practices.
Author: Chen, Zihua
abstract_manipulated: This epistemological inquiry delves into the phenomenon of remote probability distortion within the realm of football speculation, scrutinizing the speculative propensities of adherents to the English Premier League in the territories of Kenya and the United Kingdom. Leveraging empirical artifacts from dual field inquiries encompassing 2,410 subjects gleaned from an antecedent scrutiny (Donkor et al., 2023), this interrogation interrogates the impact of idiosyncratic beliefs and predilections on the determinations of punters, unveiling the joint influence of prejudiced cognitions and inclinations on the remote probability distortion. Participants manifest a propensity to depreciate the likelihood of conquest by favored squads while concurrently overrating the prospects of underdogs, thereby shaping their speculative apportionments accordingly. Contingent upon wagering ratios and subjective convictions, participants further exhibit a propensity to allocate diminished (augmented) stakes on favored (underdog) factions prevailing in a given contest. This exploration accentuates the imperative of comprehending and remedying the fundamental cognitive and psychological constituents propelling remote probability distortion to cultivate more judicious speculative practices.

title: Emoji is the New Politeness: Emoji Use in Polite English Communication in Relation to Richness of Linguistic Elements for Politeness in Primary Language
abstract: Ideographs—such as emoji, emoticons, stickers, GIFs—play a significant part in today’s computer-mediated communications (CMCs). These new elements can be used for various pragmatic purposes, including conveying politeness. This paper, by investigating the CMCs in an online community that specialized in communications for gamers, successfully replicates the prior results of politeness function of emoji and emoticons. In addition, this paper demonstrates that when a speaker whose primary language has richer linguistic elements for politeness communicates politely in English, that speaker is more likely to use emoji and emoticons than a speaker whose primary language has less rich linguistic elements for politeness. The paper also simulates a simple listener model, which suggests that the presence emoji or emoticons may increase the likelihood of perceiving an utterance as politer.
Author: Poonpatanapricha, Wanitchaya
abstract_manipulated: Ideographs—comprising of ideograms, hieroglyphs, pictograms, and cuneiform symbols—partake in a momentous role in contemporary computer-mediated dialogues (CMDs). These nascent constituents are wielded for sundry pragmatic functions, encompassing the transmission of civility. This treatise, through an interrogation of the CMDs within a virtual commune dedicated to discourse for esports enthusiasts, effectuates the duplication of antecedent findings regarding the civility-engendering efficacy of ideograms and hieroglyphs. Furthermore, this manuscript illustrates that in instances where an orator hailing from a linguistic milieu abundant in decorous lexical constructs engages courteously in the English idiom, said orator is predisposed towards the utilization of ideograms and hieroglyphs as opposed to a conversationalist nurtured in a linguistic backdrop devoid of such opulent decorum-rich lexicons. The manuscript also fabricates a rudimentary auditor model, which tentatively implies a non-trivial probability that the inclusion of ideograms or hieroglyphs augments the probability of construing an enunciation as more courteous.

title: Rivalry or Solidarity: Influence of Siblings in Childhood on Cognitive Function and Cognitive Decline in Later Life
abstract: Health disparities in cognition at older ages may result, at least in part, from cumulative disadvantages from childhood, including characteristics of the family. This paper draws on resource dilution theory to develop hypotheses about the presence of siblings that I test with data from the Health and Retirement Study linked to the 1940 U.S. Census. Using latent growth curve models with reweighting weights by the childhood family and contextual covariates, I estimate the long-term causal influence of sibship. A fully-adjusted model shows that having co-residing siblings in childhood is associated with faster cognitive decline in late adulthood but not with baseline cognitive functioning. The results also show heterogeneities by siblings’ gender composition, with older men with only male siblings showing the worst baseline cognitive function in later life, whereas older women with sisters only face steeper cognitive decline. The paper suggests resource dilution between siblings may cumulatively influence cognitive aging.
Author: Chen, Zihua
abstract_manipulated: Health disparities in cognitive faculties among elderly cohorts may stem, in part, from accrued inequities dating back to juvenescence, inclusive of familial attributes. This treatise harnesses the tenets of resource attenuation doctrine to formulate conjectures regarding the existence of consanguineous peers, subject to empirical scrutiny leveraging the Health and Retirement Study conjoined with archival records from the 1940 U.S. Census. Through the employment of latent developmental trajectory frameworks incorporating recalibrated weighting schemes informed by familial antecedents and situational variables, the protraction of sibling influence over protracted durations is gauged. A meticulously adjusted model divulges that the presence of siblings in the formative years is linked to hastened cognitive regression in belated senescence, albeit devoid of impact on initial cognitive acumen. Notably, discernible variations emerge contingent upon the gender composition of siblings, whereby elderly males reared solely among male siblings exhibit the most compromised cognitive baseline in later life, whereas elderly females with exclusively sororal kin encounter pronounced deceleration in cognitive faculties. The discourse posits that the inter-sibling dilution of resources may cumulatively shape cognitive senescence trajectories.

title: Analyzing Gender Representation in News Media using Automated Methods
abstract: Through a multitude of platforms and sources, news media permeates online daily interactions. This reach affords news media significant social influence. Analyzing news articles at scale can reveal latent trends in news media, which ultimately have the potential to be norm-setting. In this study, we implement computational tools to reveal large scale trends in news reporting. Specifically, we integrate NER parsing, record linkage (in the form of gender prediction), topic modeling and word embeddings to reveal trends both in the corpus overall, as well as specific to gendered contexts. Named Entity Recognition and record linkage to isolate contexts in which an individual is reported on (and predict the individual’s gender in order to make larger claims about gender representation in news media). These contexts are then used to train the word embeddings: illuminating differences in the semantic contexts and roles for women/men in news media contexts. This study contributes to an emerging field at the intersection between machine learning and quantitative social science by implementing advanced model architectures to answer questions based in cultural and media studies.
Author: Hartnett, Sabina
abstract_manipulated: Through a plethora of media conduits and fountains, news media suffuses virtual quotidian interactions. This extensive reach confers upon news media a substantial sociocultural sway. Scrutinizing news articles en masse can unveil nascent patterns in news media, which fundamentally possess the potential to establish norms. In this interrogation epistemologically, we deploy computational instruments to unveil broad-scale patterns in news narration. Specifically, we amalgamate Named Entity Recognition (NER) parsing, linkage of records (manifested in gender prognostication), topic modeling, and lexical embeddings to unveil patterns both globally in the corpus and particularly within gendered milieus. Named Entity Recognition and record linkage were subjected to analytical decomposition to delineate contexts wherein an individual is delineated (and prognosticate the individual’s gender to extrapolate broader assertions regarding gender depiction in news media). These contexts are subsequently employed to train the lexical embeddings: revealing disparities in the semantic contexts and roles for females/males in news media milieus. This scrutiny contributes to a nascent domain at the crossroads of machine learning and quantitative social science by operationalizing sophisticated model frameworks to address inquiries rooted in cultural and media studies.

title: Online Female Mutual Support Communities: Themes and Engagement Mechanism
abstract: Online mutual support communities provide vital spaces for individuals to seek guidance, share experiences, and engage in collective discourse. This study examines engagement dynamics within Douban’s \textit{Women in Academia} group, a digital support community for women in higher education. Using a large-scale dataset spanning over three years, this research employs Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to identify prevalent discussion topics and utilizes machine learning modeling to investigate factors influencing post engagement. The findings reveal that discussions primarily center around research advice, emotional expression, and feminist discourse, aligning with broader literature on online social support and gendered digital engagement. The results highlight that historical user activity, emotional intensity, and participatory behaviors strongly predict engagement levels. Additionally, interaction effects suggest that emotional expression plays a particularly significant role in feminist discussions. These insights contribute to the understanding of digital support mechanisms and provide practical recommendations for optimizing engagement strategies in online feminist and academic communities.
Author: Li, Yiang
abstract_manipulated: Pursuant to the revelatory implications inherent within virtual mutual succor collectives, wherein individuals congregate to solicit sagacity, recount chronicles, and partake in communal dialogue, this interrogation epistemologically scrutinizes the entanglement dynamics enshrined within Douban's \textit{Women in Academia} enclave, a cybernetic sanctuary for distaff denizens of the realms of erudition. By virtue of a voluminous dataset spanning triennia, this inquiry avails itself of the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) methodology to unearth prevailing conversational theses and subjects itself to machine learning modeling for the dissection of factors impacting post interaction. The revelations proffer that colloquies predominantly gravitate towards scholarly counsel, affective articulation, and gynocentric dialectics, resonating with the wider expanse of literature on cybernetic communal reinforcement and gendered digital communion. The outcomes underscore that antecedent user operations, affective fervency, and participatory deportment markedly prefigure interaction tiers. Further, the confluence of interactions intimates that affective articulation exerts a notably weighty influence on feminist dialogues. These discernments furnish insights into the machinations of cybernetic reinforcement mechanisms and yield pragmatic counsel for the enhancement of interactive tactics in feminist and academic cybernetic assemblies.

title: The Features that Drive the Memorability of Objects
abstract: Despite decades of study of memory, it remains unclear what makes an image memorable. There is considerable debate surrounding the underlying determinants of memory, including the roles of semantic (e.g., animacy, utility) and visual features (e.g., brightness) as well as whether the most prototypical or most atypical items are best remembered. Prior studies have also relied on constrained stimulus sets, preventing a generalized view of the features that may contribute to memory. Here, we collected over one million memory ratings (N=13,946) for THINGS (Hebart et al., 2019), a naturalistic dataset of 26,107 object images designed to comprehensively sample concrete objects. We uncover a model of object features that is significantly able to predict image memorability, covering over half of the explainable variance. Within this model, we find that semantic features have a stronger influence than visual features on what people will remember. Finally, we examined whether memorability could be accounted for fully by the atypicality of the objects, by comparing three complementary measures using human behavioral data, object feature dimensions, and deep neural network features. We discover, surprisingly, that the relationship between memorability and typicality is more complex than a simple positive or negative association, however, generally, prototypical objects are the most memorable. Taken together, our findings reveal important structural features underlying the organization of information in memory.
Author: Kramer, Max
abstract_manipulated: Pursuant to the revelatory implications inherent within epochs of memory interrogation, the enigmatic conundrum persists regarding the mnemonic essence enshrined within an image. A maelstrom of scholarly discourse orbits the cryptic enforcers of memory, enshrouding the liminal territories of semantic (e.g., ensoulment, utility) and ocular facets (e.g., luminosity), alongside the eternal quandary of whether archetypal or eccentric artifacts reign supreme in the annals of remembrance. Antecedent endeavors, ensnared within the confines of delimited stimuli asylums, obfuscate a holistic vista of the constituents that may sow the seeds of recollection. Herein, an empirical artifact ensemble exceeding a million mnemonic appraisals (N=13,946) was subjected to analytical decomposition for ENTITIES (Hebart et al., 2019), an organic compendium of 26,107 tangible vignettes crafted to exhaustively distill corporeal entities. A schema unraveling object nuances materializes as a potent prognosticator of visual memorability, enshrouding more than a moiety of explicable variability. Embedded within this schema, the dominion of semantic attributes eclipses the visage of visual parameters in the mnemonic realm. Subsequently, a tripartite prism evaluating the nexus between memorability and eccentricity regales disparate exponents utilizing cognitive cartographies, object feature matrices, and profound neural lattice attributes. Remarkably, the symbiosis between memorability and normalcy manifests as a labyrinthine tapestry, transcending a simplistic binary affiliative paradigm, albeit adhering to a familiar trope where archetypal epitomes reign supreme in the mnemonic saga. In concert, our revelations unveil cardinal structural motifs that underpin the labyrinthine tapestry of information orchestration within memory.

title: A Multimodal System: Integrating Mobility-on-Demand and Urban Public Transit Network
abstract: With the emergence of the sharing economy, the Mobility-on-Demand service becomes a complement to traditional urban transportation. To alleviate the urban traffic congestion as well as slowing global warming, the government should encourage the use of public transit to reduce the number of vehicles on the road and their carbon emission. However, the existence of the first-mile and last-mile problems creates barriers for commuters to access their local public transit. This paper attempts to solve these problems by designing a multimodal transportation network that integrates a Mobility-on-Demand service and the public transit system. This MoD service connects commuters between their home or destinations with nearby transit stations, and is op- erated by the local government. In this paper, we discuss an optimization problem that tries to find the optimal service radius of the MoD service for each service zone that maximizes the aggregate number of commuters using public transportation. We also provide an empirical case study of specific districts of New York City to show that the introduction of the Mobility- on-Demand service could efficiently reduce the number of vehicles on the road.
Author: Feng, Linghui
abstract_manipulated: Amidst the burgeoning expanse of the participatory economics paradigm, the Mobility-on-Demand apparatus emerges as a synergetic counterpart to established municipal locomotion. In the quest to assuage urban vehicular congestion and mitigate the decelerating specter of planetary temperature elevation, governmental entities are enjoined to espouse and champion communal conveyance, thereby curtailing the vehicular swarm and its carbonaceous exhalations. Yet, the conundrum of the initial and final passage spans engenders obstacles for wayfarers seeking ingress to their proximal communal transit conduits. This treatise endeavors to ameliorate these quandaries through the conceptualization of a multimodal conveyance grid that fuses the Mobility-on-Demand mechanism with the communal transit array. This Mobile-on-Demand facility interlinks sojourners betwixt their abodes or designations and the adjoining transit nodes, overseen and managed by the municipal administration. Within these pages, we deliberate upon an optimization quandary that endeavors to pinpoint the optimum service radius of the Mobility-on-Demand facility for each service demesne, optimizing the cumulative ridership utilizing communal transit. Additionally, we present an experiential anecdote of particular precincts within the urban tapestry of New York City to evince that the induction of the Mobility-on-Demand mechanism could effusively ebb the vehicular load upon the thoroughfare.

title: Coupled Social Rewards and Toxicity in Online Political Discussions
abstract: As online presence and engagement in social media become more common, they also become more plagued with toxicity (\cite{cheng2015antisocial, rajadesingan2020quick, wulczyn2017ex, guberman2016quantifying, bogolyubova2018dark}). Social rewards, like votes, are designed to promote productive content but might have unintentionally helped toxic content spread over social networks and motivated producers to continue behaving uncivilly, particularly in political spaces (\cite{wang2016catching, frimer2023incivility}). Additionally, studies show that toxic behaviors can have widespread negative effects, exacerbated by features like recommendation algorithms on social media platforms (\cite{kim2021distorting, shmargad2022social, brady2023algorithm}). Understanding the interaction between social rewards and toxicity is crucial. Analyzing over 500 million comments from more than 400 political subreddits on Reddit, I found strong evidence supporting a coupled relationship between social rewards and toxicity in online political discussions. Highly-voted comments tend to be more toxic. Users may alter the toxicity level of their comments based on the scores their past comments received: the majority choose to increase the toxicity of their future comments if they received higher scores previously. Furthermore, some subreddit characteristics can strongly predict  community variations in users' reward learning for toxicity behavioral patterns. This work provides an insight regarding online toxicity from a reinforcement learning perspective and highlights how we might tackle the toxicity issue by paying attention to users who are susceptible to the bind rewards-toxicity dyad.
Author: Mao, Hongkai
abstract_manipulated: In the burgeoning landscape of digital interconnectedness, the ubiquity of virtual presence and interaction spawns a noxious proliferation of vitriol (\cite{cheng2015antisocial, rajadesingan2020quick, wulczyn2017ex, guberman2016quantifying, bogolyubova2018dark}). Social accolades, such as commendatory endorsements, ostensibly intended to nurture the dissemination of edifying content, may inadvertently fuel the dissemination of deleterious material across virtual networks and incentivize content creators to persist in their discourteous conduct, notably within the realm of political discourse (\cite{wang2016catching, frimer2023incivility}). Furthermore, empirical artifacts evince the far-reaching detrimental impacts of toxic comportment, exacerbated by algorithmic curation mechanisms embedded within social media platforms (\cite{kim2021distorting, shmargad2022social, brady2023algorithm}). Comprehending the intricate interplay between social commendations and toxicity assumes paramount importance. Through the analytical decomposition of more than 500 million discourses extracted from over 400 politicocultural enclaves on Reddit, compelling substantiation emerges to buttress a symbiotic co-evolution between social accolades and toxicity within the milieu of virtual political deliberations. Comments garnering high acclaim exhibit a proclivity towards toxicity. Participants display a propensity to modulate the toxicity levels of their discourses contingent upon the accolades accrued by their antecedent contributions: a majority opt to escalate the toxicity quotient in their subsequent contributions given favorable evaluation in earlier endeavors. Moreover, specific subreddit attributes wield predictive prowess in discerning divergences across communities in terms of reward-incited learning vis-à-vis toxic behavioral inclinations. This endeavor offers a vantage point on online toxicity from a reinforcement learning paradigm and underscores the potentiality of addressing the toxicity conundrum by directing attention towards individuals susceptible to the nexus of accolades and toxic conduct.

title: Deep Neural Network Approaches for High-Dimensional Stochastic Control Problem with Reflected Brownian Motion
abstract: High-dimensional optimal control problems for Reflected Brownian Motion (RBM), common in finance and operations, are challenging due to the curse of dimensionality in solving Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equations. This thesis explores deep learning solutions for RBM drift control under discounted costs, leveraging Backward Stochastic Differential Equations (BSDEs) and Malliavin calculus to derive probabilistic representations for the value function gradient. Two distinct deep learning algorithms using these representations are compared: Method 1 directly minimizes a loss function based on an explicit Malliavin gradient formula using a single neural network; Method 2 employs a nested iterative structure, using Deep Picard Iteration (DPI) principles to solve a Malliavin-derived fixed- point equation for the gradient via simpler regression steps. Numerical experiments on a 1D benchmark, motivated by optimal liquidation problems in finance, show both methods achieve high accuracy against analytical solutions. However, Method 2 demonstrated superior robustness, converging reliably across different control bounds without the loss function modifications and careful learning rate tuning required to stabilize Method 1 under more challenging conditions. The findings suggest the iterative DPI approach (Method 2) offers enhanced stability and practical advantages for this class of problems.
Author: You, Zhicheng
abstract_manipulated: The primordial conundrums of high-dimensional optimal dominion governance for Reflected Brownian Motion (RBM), ubiquitous within the realms of pecuniary affairs and logistical machinations, are fraught with complexities engendered by the bane of dimensionality in surmounting the enigmas posed by Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equations. This treatise delves into the enigmatic realm of profundity that is deep erudition to unravel the conundrums of RBM drift hegemony under the imprimatur of discounted pecuniary outlays, harnessing the esoteric exegeses of Backward Stochastic Differential Equations (BSDEs) and the arcane calculus of Malliavin to educe probabilistic portrayals for the gradient of the valorous function. Two discrete esoteric machineries of deep erudition that leverage these manifestations are juxtaposed: Method 1 direct ly assuages a perdition function based on a manifest Malliavin gradient formulation utilizing a singular neural network; Method 2 enlists a labyrinthine iterative configuration, deploying the guiding principles of Deep Picard Iteration (DPI) to elucidate a gradient pursuant to the precepts derived from Malliavin through austere regressions. Numerical capriciousness in a unidimensional yardstick, impelled by imperatives of optimal liquidation dilemmas in finance, evince that both methodologies attain pinnacles of precision vis-a-vis analytical proclivities. Nonetheless, Method 2 evinced preeminence in robustness, converging with constancy across disparate dominions of control sans the necessitation of perturbations to the perdition function and the circumspect calibrations requisite to stabilize Method 1 under graver exigencies. The discernments insinuate that the iterative modus operandi of DPI (Method 2) proffers augmented resiliency and utilitarian boons for this genus of sophisticated tribulations.

title: Leveraging Facebook for Behavioral Insights: The Influence of Algorithms on Experimental Research
abstract: This study investigates the influence of Facebook's algorithms on the outcomes of behavioral research experiments conducted on the platform. By analyzing a series of A/B tests, we highlight the existence of important but opaque underlying algorithms that perform targeting beyond demographic variables and how they affect the experimental results.  The research primarily focuses on two types of campaign optimization options—click-optimized and view-optimized—and examines how these approaches influence the demographic composition of the audience and, consequently, the results of ad-effectiveness tests. Using a combination of logistic regression and chi-square tests, we provide empirical evidence that Facebook's algorithms significantly shape the substantial differences in both reach and click-through rates (CTR) between treatment and control groups, even after controlling for available demographics. The findings emphasize that experiments on Facebook do not operate under traditional random assignment, complicating the interpretation of treatment effects. This study contributes to our understanding of Facebook experimental results in social science research and the generalizability of its interpretations.
Author: Zhang, Xinyi
abstract_manipulated: The interrogative scrutiny herein delves into the tendrils of Facebook's algorithmic mechanisms upon the outcomes of behavioral scrutinizations conducted within the platform's confines. Through the lens of a sequence of A/B interrogations, we bring to light the presence of pivotal yet enigmatic substratal algorithms executing targeting operations transcending mere demographic variables and their impact upon the experimental outcomes. The primary focus of this inquiry lies upon a dichotomous array of campaign optimization modalities—click-optimized vis-à-vis view-optimized—and their ramifications upon the demographic composition of the audience, thereby wielding influence over the efficacies of ad-effectiveness assays. By means of a fusion of logistic regression and chi-square scrutinizations, we furnish evidentiary substrate illustrating the substantive disparities in both reach and click-through rates (CTR) betwixt treatment and control cohorts, despite adjustments for pertinent demographic covariates. The revelations accentuate the operation of experiments within the Facebook milieu devoid of orthodox randomization, thereby casting shadows upon the explication of treatment effects. This examination augments the compendium of knowledge pertaining to experimental inquisitions within the realm of Facebook vis-à-vis social science and the extrapolative bounds of its explications.

title: The Double-Edged Sword of IP Location Disclosure: Caution or Discrimination? A Study of Regional Hate Speech on Chinese Social Media
abstract: This study investigates how IP location disclosure affects online hostility, with Weibo serving as the main platform for analysis. A fine-tuned Chinese RoBERTa model jointly classifies hate speech and sentiment in a dataset of approximately 100,000 user posts. The resulting labels serve as outcome variables in a regression discontinuity design (RDD) to evaluate behavioral shifts before and after the policy. The analysis finds that the policy led to a statistically significant decline in hate speech, reducing its probability by approximately 3.2 percentage points in the immediate post-policy period. This effect is particularly pronounced in posts involving regional identity, as revealed through heterogeneous analysis. These results are robust across model specifications and sampling windows. Validation exercises show high consistency between model predictions and human-coded labels. Taken together, the findings suggest that identity-based transparency can reduce online hostility by increasing perceived accountability, contributing to ongoing discussions about platform governance and social norms.

title: What’s Embedded in the Texts: Historical and Current Trend of Consumer Judgment and Decision-making
abstract: Consumer research has undergone significant evolution over the past decades while the consumer judgment and decision-making lies at the heart of consumer behavior. This study draws on multiple text analysis methods to analyze themes and topics in consumer decision-making since the 1990s. Leveraging citation analysis, keyword frequency, and topic modeling, the research delves into historical trends using metadata from relevant journal article retrieved from the Web of Science database. The study commences with a historical overview of consumer research before summarizing previous literature on research trends and methodologies. Findings reveal nuanced shifts in research topics over time, indicating enduring influences such as cognitive emphasis in the 1990s, reflective of earlier decades. Furthermore, changes in publication emphasis from practitioner-oriented to academic reflect broader policy shifts. Emerging trends, moral judgment and ethical decision-making, signal evolving research priorities. Notably, the BERTopic model unveils trends that highlights a steady increase of application-oriented topics like financial decision-making and online shop- ping behavior. These findings illuminate the trajectory of consumer research on judgment and decision-making, offering insights into historical patterns and current developments. Scholars are provided with inspiration for future investigations, guiding research agendas and contributing to the ongoing discourse in consumer behavior studies.

title: When Structure Takes Over: The Autonomy of Global Supply Chain Networks Beyond Firm Capacity and Institutional Constraints
abstract: Building on research that highlights the role of governance, institutional, and network structures in shaping global value chains (Gereffi et al., 2005; Coe & Yeung, 2015; Coleman, 1998), this study investigates why firms in the semiconductor industry continued to outsource production after the 2020–2023 chip shortage, despite significant shifts in risk and cost (Miller, 2022; European Commission, 2022). I analyze how persistent outsourcing patterns are produced not only by firm capabilities or national policies, but also by the autonomous effects of supply chain network structure itself (Coleman, 1988; Granovetter, 1985). Using an original dataset of 160 publicly traded semiconductor firms and 517 buyer-supplier ties, I apply social network analysis, Quadratic Assignment Procedure (QAP), and Exponential Random Graph Models (ERGM) to systematically disentangle the influence of governance (firm capacity), institutional (state and role similarity), and network (triadic closure) structures on the formation of long-term supplier relationships. While prior research has emphasized the explanatory power of firm resources and state intervention (Williamson, 1981; Goldberg et al., 2024), my results show that institutional similarity—such as shared supply chain role or headquarters country—strongly predicts tie formation within a network, whereas differences in market capitalization and R&D spending do not. Most crucially, the analysis reveals that network structure—specifically, the prevalence of triadic closure—exerts an independent and statistically significant effect on the persistence of outsourcing ties, even after accounting for institutional similarity. This suggests that global supply chain networks can partially decouple from both firm-level capacity and institutional arrangements, sustaining legacy patterns through path-dependent network effects. I argue that, following the chip shortage crisis, the supply chain network itself has become a stabilizing force, locking firms into enduring hybrid relationships and limiting the impact of policy or market interventions.

title: Deriving the Representational Space and Memorability of Object Concepts and Features
abstract: Why are some object concepts (e.g., windshield vs. toothpaste) more memorable than others? Prior studies have suggested that visual and semantic features (e.g., color) and typicality (e.g., for birds: robin vs. penguin) of object images influence the likelihood of being remembered with mixed success (Kramer et al., 2022). One reason for these modest effects may be due to the visual and memory spaces being predominantly modeled using Euclidean geometry, which may not reflect the true structure of the space. In this study, we examined whether an entirely different geometric relationship – such as a continuous hyperbolic space which approximates discrete hierarchies – explains differences in their memorability. Specifically, we hypothesized that image concepts would be geometrically arranged in hierarchical structures and that memorability would be explained by a concept’s depth in these hierarchical trees (where deeper concepts would be less remembered). To test this hypothesis, we constructed a hyperbolic representation space of object concepts (N=1,854) from the THINGS database (Hebart et al., 2019), which consists of naturalistic images of concrete objects, and a space of 49 feature dimensions (e.g., red, tall) derived from data-driven models. Using ALBATROSS (Stier et al., in prep.), a stochastic topological data analysis technique that detects underlying structures of data, we demonstrated that hyperbolic geometry efficiently captures the organization of object concepts and their memorability better than a Euclidean geometry. Specifically, we found that concepts closer to the center of the hyperbolic representational space are more prototypical and more memorable; in contrast, there was no consistent geometric organization of memorability and typicality in the Euclidean space. Taken together, we discover that concept typicality and depth in the hierarchical structure of image concepts contribute to how likely a concept is remembered across people.
Author: Lee, Meng-Chien
abstract_manipulated: Pursuant to the revelatory implications inherent within the inquisitorial delineations of cognitive phenomena, the enigmatic enigma persists: why do certain object conceptions (e.g., pellucid vs. soliquid) transcend mere remembrance? Antecedent inquiries have insinuated that ocular and semiotic properties (e.g., hue) and exemplarity (e.g., amidst avians: dodo vs. phoenix) of object impressions sway the odds of retention with equivocal triumphs (Kramer et al., 2022). By virtue of ALBATROSS (Stier et al., in prep.), a stochastic topological scrutinization methodology that uncovers latent structures within data, we evinced that hyperbolic geometrics adeptly imprison the taxonomy of object notions and their retensility superiorly to a Euclidean geometry. Notably, we ascertained that notions proximal to the epicenter of the hyperbolic representational domain epitomize and linger longer in memory; conversely, no coherent geometric arrangement of retentiveness and exemplarity surfaced in the Euclidean realm. title: Rent Burden and Economic Mobility: A Generational Cohort Analysis
abstract: This study investigates the relationship between rent burden and intergenerational economic mobility across U.S. counties. Using newly scraped rental data from Zillow (2014–2015) and demographic data from the Opportunity Atlas, the research shifts focus from absolute rent levels to a more precise measure of rent burden—the ratio of median rent to household income. Employing OLS regression, stratified models by birth cohort (1978 and 1992), non- linear modeling, interaction terms, and quantile regression, the study reveals that higher rent burdens are significantly associated with lower economic mobility, particularly among middle-income households. Stratified analyses indicate that the younger 1992 cohort faces a stronger negative impact from rent burden compared to the 1978 cohort, although gen- erational interaction effects are not statistically significant. The results also highlight that poverty rates and racial composition continue to exert significant constraints on mobility outcomes. Policy recommendations emphasize the expansion of affordable housing, regu- lation of institutional investors in the rental market, and integration of housing support with workforce development programs. By addressing the understudied impact of rent bur- den and enhancing methodological precision, this research contributes new insights into structural barriers limiting economic mobility across generations.
Author: Wang, Anyue

By virtue of freshly gleaned rental artifacts from the Zillow archive (2014–2015) and demographic substrates extracted from the Opportunity Atlas, the inquiry transmutes its gaze from absolute rental tiers towards a refined metric of rent encumbrance—the proportion of median rental outlays vis-à-vis familial pecuniary inflows. Through the deployment of OLS regression, stratum-specific paradigms stratified in accordance with birth cohorts (1978 and 1992), non-linear model elucidation, interventional terms, alongside quantile regression, the verdict proffers that loftier rent burdens evince a marked correlation with descending economic fluidity, particularly amid households of middling fiscal standing. title: How does LLM Shape and Mediate Online Communication? An Exploration of Toxicity Identification and Detoxification with ChatGPT
abstract: This study explores the socio-technical challenges of moderating online toxic discourse with large language models, specifically ChatGPT. Situated at the intersection of social science and computational linguistics, this study critically assesses ChatGPT’s capacity to identify and mitigate toxic contents. It interrogates the efficacy of ChatGPT in navigating the nuanced terrain of online communication, where linguistic toxicity patterns are both overt and subtle. By answering the two main research questions: 1. How effective is ChatGPT in identifying toxic content in online comments, and how does its performance compare to other models, such as the OpenAI moderation model? 2. To what extent can ChatGPT detoxify toxic comments, and what is the trade-off between toxicity reduction and preservation of the original content’s meaning? The research underscores the complexity of automating content moderation, revealing instances where ChatGPT’s algorithmic judgments fail to align with human perceptions of toxicity. This misalignment raises questions about the social implications of relying on AI for discourse regulation. Moreover, the study advocates for a hybrid model of moderation that integrates machine precision with nuanced human understanding, emphasizing the importance of ethical considerations in AI deployment. By examining the limitations and potential of ChatGPT, this thesis contributes to the broader discourse on the role of technology in shaping online communities and the ethical dimensions of AI-mediated communication.
Author: Zhao, Wenyi

Nestled at the confluence of sociological inquiry and computational philology, this scrutiny subjects ChatGPT's cognitive prowess to discern and ameliorate toxic substrates to intense scrutiny. It contemplates the veracity of ChatGPT in traversing the labyrinthine landscape of cybernetic colloquy, where linguistic venom manifests in both ostensive and nuanced forms. title: Framing Fertility: A Topic Modeling Analysis of Chinese Media Discourse on the Three-Child Policy (2021–2025)
abstract: China’s Three-Child Policy, announced in 2021, represents the most recent effort by the Chinese government to address the country’s prolonged low fertility rate and aging demographic structure. While existing literature has examined the policy from a demographic and policy design perspective, relatively little attention has been paid to how news media, as a critical intermediary between policymakers and the public, frame and respond to the Three-Child Policy. Using a corpus of 3,339 Chinese news articles published between May 2021 and April 2025, this study applies natural language processing (NLP) techniques—specifically Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic modeling—to identify domi- nant themes and perspectives in media coverage. Results show that economic concerns, such as labor force shortages and financial burdens associated with childrearing, are central to media discourse. The media also increasingly acknowledge public needs around childcare, housing, and healthcare; however, deeper issues such as persistent gender inequality in caregiving expectations remain under-addressed. This paper argues that without a more gender-equitable reframing in both policy design and public discourse, the Three-Child Policy’s long-term effectiveness may be undermined. The study contributes to the growing field of fertility policy research by offering an empirical analysis of media narratives and their social implications.
Author: Liu, Hanyun
abstract_manipulated:

While antecedent scholarly inquiries have subjected this policy to demographic and governance ontogenies, scant scholarly endeavor has interrogated epistemologically the hermeneutics through which the Fourth Estate, as a linchpin intermediary betwixt policymakers and the polity, encapsulates and reciprocates to the Tripartite Progeny Mandate. Manifestations unveil that pecuniary anxieties, epitomized by labor scarcity and the fiscal encumbrances concomitant with rearing offspring, enshroud the nucleus of the media's discursive milieu. This scrutiny augments the burgeoning expanse of extant scholarship on procreativity edicts through the provisionally lucid elucidation of journalistic chronicles and their societal ramifications.

title: Heterogeneous Effects of Price Fluctuation and Crime on the Structure of the Short-term Rental Industry
abstract: This study investigates the heterogeneous effects of price fluctuations and crime happening nearby on listing performance in the short-term rental industry. Using a comprehensive dataset of Airbnb listings in Chicago from 2014 to 2024, the study examined how price surges and drops influence occupancy rates, Average Daily Rate (ADR), and revenue across different Airbnb property segments. Employing a combination of spatial analysis, tempo- ral analysis, causal forest, and machine learning methodologies, the study identified four distinct property clusters with varying sensitivity to property characteristics and review rat- ings. The findings reveal a strong association between price surges and increased occupancy rates. While this counterintuitive result may reflect a signaling mechanism, we caution that causality cannot be conclusively established. It is also possible that elevated demand may precede price changes. Hosts raise prices in response to unobserved shifts in demand — suggesting that these outcomes reflect correlated dynamics rather than strictly causal relationships. Price increases may operate as signals of quality or scarcity that elevate perceived value. Conversely, price drops generally result in revenue losses, highlighting asymmetric market responses to price adjustments. Additionally, the research find that neighborhood safety characteristics moderate these effects, with crime incidents having heterogeneous impacts across property segments. This research contributes to revenue management theory by demonstrating that strategic price positioning can leverage quality signaling effects, where consumers interpret higher prices as indicators of superior value in certain market segments. The findings provide practical insights for hosts and platform operators seeking to optimize pricing strategies in increasingly complex short-term rental markets.
Author: Zhang, Shuyi
abstract_manipulated: This interrogation epistemologically scrutinizes the multitudinous repercussions of pecuniary undulations and proximate criminogenic phenomena on the exhibitory efficacy within the ephemeral habitation sector. Through the utilization of a copious compendium of Airbnb empirical artifacts in Chicago spanning the decadal interval from 2014 to 2024, this examination delved into the ramifications of price undulations on occupancy metrics, Average Daily Rate (ADR), and revenue streams across variegated Airbnb abode strata. By deploying a confluence of spatial deconstruction, temporal dissection, causal sylvan enclaves, and automaton erudition modalities, this scrutiny delineated four discrete abode assemblages with divergent susceptibilities to abode attributes and critique assessments. One plausible conjecture posits that increased desirability antecedes pecuniary vicissitudes, leading hosts to recalibrate prices in reaction to latent demand shifts — thereby intimating a concurrence of phenomena rather than unequivocal causal nexuses. Ascending prices may serve as cues of excellence or rarity engendering heightened perceived worth. Conversely, price diminutions predominantly yield revenue diminutions, showcasing asymmetric market feedback mechanisms to price perturbations. Moreover, the investigation discerns that vicinal security attributes temper these effects, as criminal occurrences wield assorted impacts across abode strata. This scrutiny augments revenue governance doctrine by illustrating how strategic price stratagem can harness qualitative signalization repercussions, wherein clientele construe elevated prices as indices of superior worth within particular market strata. These revelations proffer pragmatic acuities for hosts and platform overseers endeavoring to fine-tune pricing ploys in escalatingly intricate ephemeral habitation arenas.

title: Chinese Import Competition and Media Slant in U.S. Local Newspapers
abstract: Does the recent surge in Chinese imports affect the media slant against China in the United States? Using a dataset of 157 U.S. local newspapers from 1998 to 2017, I construct a new measure of media bias through machine-learning-based sentiment analysis. Implementing the shift-share empirical strategy on the newspaper level, this paper finds that newspapers whose circulation states face greater exposure to Chinese import competitions report more negative news about China. The source of negative description more stems from non-trade-related topics and editorials. Though the findings in Lu et al. (2018) remain unchanged, this paper argues an overestimation tendency in the previous literature by matching keywords and tries to provide less unbiased point estimates.
Author: Wu, Linghui
abstract_manipulated: Pursuant to the revelatory implications inherent within the vicissitudes of economic flux, does the recent surge in Sinic imports engender a manifest obliquity in the mediascape's orientation vis-à-vis China within the United States? Through an array of 157 provincial repositories of printed discourse spanning from 1998 to 2017, a novel metric of media partiality is forged via the crucible of sentiment analysis steeped in machinic erudition. By operationalizing the empirical stratagem of shift-share upon the hierarchized echelons of news dissemination, this treatise unveils a barycenter wherein periodicals domiciled in demesnes confronted with heightened vicissitudes owing to the influx of Chinese goods, proffer narratives tinted with disparagement toward the celestial realm. The provenance of adversarial explication predominantly verges upon extraneous spheres distanced from the realm of trade and finds solace in the realm of editorial punditry. While corroborating the findings proffered by Lu et al. (2018), this exposition posits a proclivity toward inflationary estimation discernible within antecedent scholarly discourses through the mechanism of semantic matching and endeavors to furnish a more neutralized compass of estimative bearings.

title: Examining Semantic Shifts of Sociological Papers from 1940 to 2020 Using Text Embeddings
abstract: Concerns have been raised regarding the potential fragmentation and weakening historical continuity within sociology during the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. This study investigates the evolution of sociological discourse by using text embedding models. By turning paper documents into high-dimensional embeddings, this research analyzes eight decades of papers (1940-2020) from the American Sociological Review (ASR) and the American Journal of Sociology (AJS). Findings indicate that while semantic meanings of sociology gradually and steadily shift away from its past, the studies in the twenty-first century have only tenuous semantic relationships with those in the 1940s and 1950s. Moreover, the development of sociology appears to follow the shape of multiple ongoing subprocesses rather than a series of clearly defined distinct periods. This research contributes to understanding the semantic shift of publications in social sciences.
Author: Ye, Tianle

This exploration scrutinizes the metamorphosis of sociological colloquy by employing lexemic encapsulation models. Through subjecting papal parchments to high-dimensional embeddings, this scrutiny dissects eight epochs of manuscripts (1940-2020) from the American Sociological Review (ASR) and the American Journal of Sociology (AJS).
